Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-10-31T19:13:46Z","updated_at":"2024-11-06T13:03:32Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

When `a` and `v` are structured arrays it is possible for searchsorted to give unexpected results.  Unfortunately, my reproducer involves an ~80 MiB array which I am unable to upload (but can share).[](url)

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

data = np.load('data.npz')
conv, pcon = data['conv'], data['pcon']

print('conv', conv.shape, conv.dtype)
print('pcon', pcon.shape, pcon.dtype)

# Batch search
idx_b = np.searchsorted(conv, pcon)

# Scalar search
idx_s = []
for c in pcon:
    idx_s.append(np.searchsorted(conv, c))

print('Check:', np.allclose(idx_b, np.array(idx_s)))
print('Batch:', pcon[2], idx_b[2], conv[idx_b[2]])
print('Scalar:', pcon[2], idx_s[2], conv[idx_s[2]])
```

### Error message:

The output from this script is:
```shell
conv (26135167,) [('l', '<i8'), ('r', '<i8')]
pcon (79185,) [('l', '<i8'), ('r', '<i8')]
Check: False
Batch: (6708067, 6789603) 20198971 (6708068, 6708900)
Scalar: (6708067, 6789603) 20198969 (6708067, 6789603)

```
where one can see the sizes/types of the arrays and the fact that the scalar approach (calling searchsorted for each element in `pcon`) gives different results to the batch approach (passing in pcon directly to a single searchsorted call).  Moreover, in this case every element of `pcon` is present in `conv`, as such the output from the final two print statements shows that the batch approach is in error:

### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.11.10 and NumPy 2.1.0","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27682/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27682/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2459700103","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27682#issuecomment-2459700103","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27682","id":2459700103,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86SnAuH","user":{"login":"FreddieWitherden","id":528611,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjUyODYxMQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/528611?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/FreddieWitherden","html_url":"https://github.com/FreddieWitherden","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FreddieWitherden/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FreddieWitherden/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FreddieWitherden/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FreddieWitherden/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FreddieWitherden/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FreddieWitherden/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FreddieWitherden/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FreddieWitherden/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/FreddieWitherden/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-06T13:03:32Z","updated_at":"2024-11-06T13:03:32Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> @FreddieWitherden we really need a follow-up on this to do something. Right now, it is very unclear if there may be a serious bug with no way to try and reproduce it.
> 
> I at least don't see anything obviously wrong related to it (although the structured dtype comparison code is daunting).

Apologies for the delay I've been on travel and can't currently VPN into my workstation.

I'll need to confirm but I *think* the array might not be properly sorted as I stumbled upon this issue while debugging another issue with our code.  Saying that, I would still expect a binary search (scalar or vector) to give the same answer (even if it is not 'correct' due to the array being not properly sorted).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2459700103/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2024-10-31T19:13:46Z","updated_at":"2024-11-06T13:23:43Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

When `a` and `v` are structured arrays it is possible for searchsorted to give unexpected results.  Unfortunately, my reproducer involves an ~80 MiB array which I am unable to upload (but can share).[](url)

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

data = np.load('data.npz')
conv, pcon = data['conv'], data['pcon']

print('conv', conv.shape, conv.dtype)
print('pcon', pcon.shape, pcon.dtype)

# Batch search
idx_b = np.searchsorted(conv, pcon)

# Scalar search
idx_s = []
for c in pcon:
    idx_s.append(np.searchsorted(conv, c))

print('Check:', np.allclose(idx_b, np.array(idx_s)))
print('Batch:', pcon[2], idx_b[2], conv[idx_b[2]])
print('Scalar:', pcon[2], idx_s[2], conv[idx_s[2]])
```

### Error message:

The output from this script is:
```shell
conv (26135167,) [('l', '<i8'), ('r', '<i8')]
pcon (79185,) [('l', '<i8'), ('r', '<i8')]
Check: False
Batch: (6708067, 6789603) 20198971 (6708068, 6708900)
Scalar: (6708067, 6789603) 20198969 (6708067, 6789603)

```
where one can see the sizes/types of the arrays and the fact that the scalar approach (calling searchsorted for each element in `pcon`) gives different results to the batch approach (passing in pcon directly to a single searchsorted call).  Moreover, in this case every element of `pcon` is present in `conv`, as such the output from the final two print statements shows that the batch approach is in error:

If it isn't sorted would it be possible to tweak the documentation as the current comment:

> This function uses the same algorithm as the builtin python [bisect.bisect_left](https://docs.python.org/3/library/bisect.html#bisect.bisect_left) (side='left') and [bisect.bisect_right](https://docs.python.org/3/library/bisect.html#bisect.bisect_right) (side='right') functions, which is also vectorized in the v argument.

threw me off. Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-10-31T19:13:46Z","updated_at":"2024-10-31T19:13:46Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

When `a` and `v` are structured arrays it is possible for searchsorted to give unexpected results.  Unfortunately, my reproducer involves an ~80 MiB array which I am unable to upload (but can share).[](url)

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

data = np.load('data.npz')
conv, pcon = data['conv'], data['pcon']

print('conv', conv.shape, conv.dtype)
print('pcon', pcon.shape, pcon.dtype)

# Batch search
idx_b = np.searchsorted(conv, pcon)

# Scalar search
idx_s = []
for c in pcon:
    idx_s.append(np.searchsorted(conv, c))

print('Check:', np.allclose(idx_b, np.array(idx_s)))
print('Batch:', pcon[2], idx_b[2], conv[idx_b[2]])
print('Scalar:', pcon[2], idx_s[2], conv[idx_s[2]])
```


### Error message:

```shell
The output from this script is:

conv (26135167,) [('l', '<i8'), ('r', '<i8')]
pcon (79185,) [('l', '<i8'), ('r', '<i8')]
Check: False
Batch: (6708067, 6789603) (6708068, 6708900)
Scalar: (6708067, 6789603) (6708067, 6789603)
```
where one can see the sizes/types of the arrays and the fact that the scalar approach (calling searchsorted for each element in `pcon`) gives different results to the batch approach (passing in pcon directly to a single searchsorted call).  Moreover, in this case every element of `pcon` is present in `conv`, as such the output from the final two print statements shows that the batch approach is in error:
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.11.10 and NumPy 2.1.0
